Progress in physics and chemistry proceeded much faster than in Biology. Applications of physics and chemistry in our daily life also have... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: M.S. SWAMINATHAN, CHAPTER 7, HUMAN HEALTH AND DISEASE, 7.1 Common Diseases in. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Human Health and Disease is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 7 Human Health and Disease Chapter 8 Microbes in Human Welfare Biology is the youngest of the formalised disciplines of natural science.

The text explains that progress in physics and chemistry proceeded much faster than in Biology.

The text explains that applications of physics and chemistry in our daily life also have a higher visibility than those of biology.

The text explains that however, twentieth century and certainly twenty-first century has demonstrated the utility of biological knowledge in furthering human welfare, be it in health sector or agriculture.

The text explains that the discovery of antibiotics, and synthetic plant-derived drugs, anaesthetics have changed medical practice on one hand and human health on the other hand.

The text explains that life expectancy of human beings have dramatically changed over the years.

The text explains that agricultural practices, food processing and diagnostics have brought socio-cultural changes in human communities.

The text explains that these are briefly described in the following three chapters of this unit.

The text explains that born in August 1925 in Kumbakonam in Tamil Nadu, Monkambu Sambasivan Swaminathan did his graduation and post-graduation in Botany from Madras University.

The text explains that he worked in different capacities in large number of institutions in India and abroad and developed his expertise in genetics and plant breeding.

The text explains that the School of Cytogenetics and Radiation Research established at the Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI) enabled Swaminathan and his team to develop short-duration high-yielding varieties of rice including scented Basmati.

The text explains that he is also known for the development of the concept of crop cafeteria, crop scheduling and genetically improving the yield and quality.

The text explains that swaminathan initiated collaboration with Norman Borlaug, which culminated in the ‘Green Revolution’ through introduction of Mexican varieties of wheat in India.

The text explains that he is also the initiator of ‘Lab-to-Land’, food security and several other environmental programmes.

The text explains that he has been honoured with Padma Bhushan and several other prestigious awards, medals and fellowships by institutions of excellence.

The text explains that health, for a long time, was considered as a state of body and mind where there was a balance of certain ‘humors’.

The text explains that this is what early Greeks like Hippocrates as well as Indian Ayurveda system of medicine asserted.

The text explains that the discovery of blood circulation by William Harvey using experimental method and the demonstration of normal body temperature in persons with blackbile using thermometer disproved the ‘good humor’ hypothesis of health.

The text explains that in later years, biology stated that mind influences, through neural system and endocrine system, our immune system and that our immune system maintains our health.

The text explains that cHAPTER 7 HUMAN HEALTH AND DISEASE 7.1 Common Diseases in Humans 7.2 Immunity 7.3 AIDS 7.4 Cancer 7.5 Drugs and Alcohol Abuse

The text explains that awareness about diseases and their effect on different bodily functions, vaccination (immunisation) against infectious diseases, proper disposal of wastes, control of vectors and maintenance of hygiene in food and water resources are necessary for achieving good health.

The text explains that when the functioning of one or more organs or systems of the body is adversely affected, characterised by appearance of various signs and symptoms, we say that we are not healthy, i.e., we have a disease.

The text explains that droplets resulting from cough or sneezes of an infected person are either inhaled directly or transmitted through contaminated objects such as pens, books, cups, doorknobs, computer keyboard or mouse, etc., and cause infection in a healthy person.

The text explains that drinking water and food contaminated by the faecal matter are the main source of infection.

The text explains that biotechnology (about which you will read more in Chapter 10) is at the verge of making available newer and safer vaccines.

The text explains that this overall ability of the host to fight the disease-causing organisms, conferred by the immune system is called immunity.

The text explains that grafts from Figure 7.4 Structure of an antibody molecule

The text explains that 136 BIOLOGY just any source – an animal, another primate, or any human beings cannot be made since the grafts would be rejected sooner or later.

The text explains that when ready-made antibodies are directly given to protect the body against foreign agents, it is called passive immunity.

The text explains that 7.2.4 Vaccination and Immunisation The principle of immunisation or vaccination is based on the property of ‘memory’ of the immune system.

The text explains that if a person is infected with some deadly microbes to which quick immune response is required as in tetanus, we need to directly inject the preformed antibodies, or antitoxin (a preparation containing antibodies to the toxin).

The text explains that hUMAN HEALTH AND DISEASE 137 7.2.5 Allergies When you have gone to a new place and suddenly you started sneezing, wheezing for no explained reason, and when you went away, your symptoms dissappeared.

The text explains that the exaggerated response of the immune system to certain antigens present in the environment is called allergy.

The text explains that two, sometimes, due to genetic and other unknown reasons, the body attacks self-cells.

The text explains that rheumatoid arthritis which affects many people in our society is an auto-immune disease.

The text explains that 7.2.7 Immune System in the Body The human immune system consists of lymphoid organs, tissues, cells and soluble molecules like antibodies.

The text explains that as you have read, immune system is unique in the sense that it recognises foreign antigens, responds to these and remembers them.

The text explains that the immune system also plays an important role in allergic reactions, auto-immune diseases and organ transplantation.

The text explains that the lymph nodes are small solid structures located at different points along the lymphatic system.

The text explains that this means deficiency of immune system, acquired during the lifetime of an individual indicating that it is not a congenital disease. ‘Syndrome’ means a group of symptoms.

The text explains that it is, hence, imperative, for the physical and psychological well-being, that the HIV/AIDS infected persons are not isolated from family and society.

The text explains that this viral DNA gets incorporated into host cell’s DNA and directs the infected cells to produce virus particles (Figure 7.6).

The text explains that the only excuse may be ignorance and it has been rightly said – “don’t die of ignorance”.

The text explains that in our country the National AIDS Control Organisation (NACO) and other non-governmental organisation (NGOs) are doing a lot to educate people about AIDS.

The text explains that hIV/AIDS-infected people need help and sympathy instead of being shunned by society.

The text explains that unless society recognises it as a problem to be dealt with in a collective manner – the chances of wider spread of the disease increase manifold.

The text explains that it is a malady that can only be tackled, by the society and medical fraternity acting together, to prevent the spread of the disease.

The text explains that 142 BIOLOGY Figure 7.7 Chemical structure of Morphine Figure 7.8 Opium poppy applied to detect genes in individuals with inherited susceptibility to certain cancers.

The text explains that tumor cells have been shown to avoid detection and destruction by immune system.

The text explains that differentiate the following and give examples of each: (a) Innate and acquired immunity (b) Active and passive immunity 9.

The text explains that draw a well-labelled diagram of an antibody molecule.

The text explains that what are the various routes by which transmission of human immuno- deficiency virus takes place?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7: Human Health and Disease

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
